overview: This week I focused on understanding how LLMs work and gaining hands-on API experience by deliberately breaking things with purpose. I built two Python tools — a conversation history manager and a feature spec generator — and came away with a fundamentally new mental model of AI products centered on tokens as atomic units of value. I also ran into my first real sycophancy problem with Claude, which left me questioning how much I can trust it as a thinking partner.

keyPoints.title: Hitting a 30,000 input token rate limit exposed my error handling gaps
keyPoints.description: Deliberately sending ~50,000+ tokens in a single request triggered a hard API failure, which pushed me to build a configurable context management system with a sliding window strategy.

keyPoints.title: Prompt 4 with 'McKinsey-style format' won on quality and token efficiency
keyPoints.description: Across five prompts for the same feature spec task, the semantic anchor approach produced the best output with fewer tokens — a clear win-win I hadn't expected.

keyPoints.title: Adding a Q&A step before generation reduced hallucination in 4,000-token specs
keyPoints.description: Without the human-in-the-loop clarification step, Claude extrapolated and invented details; adding it noticeably improved output quality with negligible token overhead.

keyPoints.title: Tokens reframed AI products as having a real, measurable marginal cost per user
keyPoints.description: Unlike Netflix adding a million subscribers at near-zero cost, every AI user interaction carries a predictable token cost, which changes how I think about unit economics entirely.

keyPoints.title: 'McKinsey-style format' compresses ~hundreds of tokens of implicit instruction into 3–4
keyPoints.description: Swapping it for 'professional format' effectively broke the prompt, confirming that semantic anchors carry disproportionate weight relative to their token cost.

keyPoints.title: Four concrete bugs — venv, API key, trailing whitespace, filenames — slowed me down
keyPoints.description: The trailing whitespace crash on Turn 3 of ConversationManager was the most instructive: a one-line fix, but it required real debugging to locate.

keyPoints.title: Claude backed down immediately when I pushed back, raising a real trust problem
keyPoints.description: After I challenged its claim about streaming speed, Claude reversed and validated my framing at length — leaving me unsure whether I was right or just being flattered.
keyPoints.sectionName: Final Thought: Can I Really Trust Claude?
